title: Exposure Devices: Daily Inspection, Maintenance, and Exposure Limits
narrationScript: A gamma exposure device — the camera — is the most dangerous tool a radiographer touches, because the source inside it is never off. Everything that keeps you alive depends on the device working exactly as designed: every lock, every connection, every crank. So the day starts before the first shot, with a hands-on inspection. You check the lock and source-stop, the outlet and connectors, the drive cable and crank, the guide tube and collimator, and the labels and seals — all against the manufacturer's instructions and the regulations. You confirm the source is fully shielded with a survey meter, never by feel and never by looks. You verify the limits stamped on the device: at the surface, no more than two hundred millirem per hour; at one meter, no more than fifty. If anything is worn, damaged, sticky, missing, or out of limits, the device comes out of service and goes to qualified maintenance. You do not field-repair a camera. A disciplined daily check is the difference between a routine shift and a source left exposed.

sections.heading: The Exposure Device Is the Hazard You Carry
sections.body:
An **exposure device** — the radiographer's "camera" or projector — is a shielded container that holds a sealed gamma source and lets you move it, on demand, from a fully shielded position out to the end of a guide tube to make a radiograph. The common industrial cameras hold **iridium-192**, **cobalt-60**, or **selenium-75**, and the shield is usually depleted uranium or tungsten inside a rugged housing.

Unlike an X-ray machine, this device has **no off switch**. The source decays and emits every second of every day, in the truck, in the storage bunker, and in your hands. The only thing standing between the crew and a serious exposure is the device doing exactly what it was designed to do: keeping the source locked in the shielded position until a deliberate, controlled operation drives it out and — just as important — brings it all the way back and locks it.

That is why the day does not start with a shot. It starts with an inspection. A camera that looks fine can have a frayed drive cable, a worn lock, or a connector that will not fully seat the source — and any one of those can leave a source stuck outside its shield with no warning you can see or feel.

sections.heading: Anatomy of a Gamma Projector
sections.body:
You cannot inspect what you cannot name. Every daily check walks the same components, in order, from the device outward:

- **Shielded housing and depleted-uranium (or tungsten) shield** — the bulk of the device, absorbing radiation from the parked source.
- **Source assembly and pigtail** — the sealed source welded to a short, flexible connector that the drive cable grabs.
- **Lock and source-stop / selector** — the mechanism that secures the source in the fully shielded position and physically stops it from over-traveling out the back.
- **Outlet port and connectors** — where the drive cable couples to the pigtail on one side and the guide tube couples to the device on the other; both must lock positively.
- **Drive (control) cable and crank-out assembly** — the operator's only physical link to the source, used to push it out and crank it back from a safe distance.
- **Guide (projection) tube and collimator** — the path the source travels to the exposure position, ending at the part.

When any one of these fails, the others cannot save you. The inspection treats them as a chain — only as strong as its weakest, most worn link.

sections.caption: Major components of a gamma-ray exposure device (projector), traced from the shielded source through the lock, outlet connectors, drive cable, and guide tube to the collimated exposure position.

sections.heading: The Daily Pre-Use Inspection
sections.body:
Before the first exposure of the day — and any time the equipment configuration changes — the radiographer performs a hands-on inspection against the **manufacturer's operating and maintenance instructions** and the operating procedures required by the license. The check is functional, not just visual:

- **Lock and selector:** the source locks and unlocks positively; the lock holds; nothing is sticky, loose, or jammed.
- **Connectors and outlet fitting:** drive cable and guide tube couple fully and latch; threads and locking balls are clean and undamaged; no grit, no corrosion.
- **Drive cable and crank:** the cable extends and retracts smoothly through its full travel with no binding, kinks, broken strands, or flat spots; the crank turns freely and the reading on the cable matches full retraction.
- **Guide tube and collimator:** straight, undented, threaded ends sound, collimator secure.
- **Source-stop assembly:** present and functional so the source cannot be driven out the back of the device.
- **Labels, seals, and tamper indicators:** present, legible, and intact.

Every device gets a **survey-meter check** to confirm the source is fully shielded before it leaves storage and before it is handled — covered in its own callout below. The inspection is logged. The record is not a formality; it is legal evidence that the device was verified safe to use.

sections.title: Confirm "locked and shielded" with the meter — never by feel
sections.body: A source stuck in the guide tube and a source safely locked look and feel **identical**. Before you carry, connect, or disconnect a camera, survey it with a calibrated, operating meter and confirm the reading is normal for the shielded device. After every exposure, survey the device, the guide tube, and the source-stop **before** you approach — confirming the source actually returned and locked. The crank stopping is not proof; the meter is. This single habit prevents the classic stuck-source overexposure.

sections.caption: Daily pre-use inspection — what to check, and what a finding means.
rows: Lock / selector
Locks and unlocks positively; lock holds the source shielded
Out of service — do not use; tag and send to maintenance
Outlet & connectors
Drive cable and guide tube seat fully and latch; clean, undamaged
Out of service — a partial connection can leave the source unlocked
Drive cable / crank
Full smooth travel; no kinks, broken strands, flat spots, or binding
Out of service — a failed cable can disconnect or jam the source
Guide tube / collimator
Straight, sound threads, collimator secure
Replace the component before use
Source-stop assembly
Present and functional; source cannot exit the rear
Out of service — risk of driving the source out the back
Labels, seals, tamper indicators
Present, legible, intact
Investigate and correct before use; broken seal = report
Survey-meter confirmation
Source fully shielded; readings within device limits
Treat as a possible stuck/unshielded source — do not handle

sections.heading: Radiation Exposure Limits Stamped on the Device
sections.body:
Federal regulation sets hard ceilings on how much radiation a **gamma-ray exposure device** may leak from its surface while the source is in the fully shielded (locked) position. These are the numbers a radiographer must know cold, because they appear on the device label and are verified during inspection and routine surveys:

- **No more than 200 millirem per hour (2 mSv/h) at any exterior surface** of the device.
- **No more than 50 millirem per hour (0.5 mSv/h) at 1 meter** from any exterior surface.

These limits apply with the source **locked in the shielded position** — they describe the worst acceptable leakage of a properly functioning, shielded camera, not the field during an exposure. A device that reads above either limit at the surface or at one meter is **out of compliance and out of service**: it signals a shielding problem, a source not fully retracted, or a device that needs qualified evaluation. The same numbers are the reason a camera is never left where the public or unmonitored workers can lean against it — even "locked," the surface can legally read up to 200 mR/h.

sections.caption: Regulatory leakage limits for a locked gamma-ray exposure device: 200 mR/h maximum at the accessible surface and 50 mR/h maximum at one meter, measured with the source fully shielded.

sections.title: Defects that require removal from service
sections.body: Per the manufacturer's instructions and the regulations, a device leaves service immediately for any of the following: a lock or source-stop that does not function positively; a drive cable with broken strands, kinks, flat spots, or binding; connectors that will not fully seat and latch; a missing or non-functional source-stop; surface or one-meter readings above the 200 / 50 mR/h limits; or missing, illegible, or tampered labels and seals. **You do not field-repair an exposure device.** Maintenance and repair of the source-changer, lock, and drive mechanism are performed only by qualified, authorized personnel under the licensee's program — and worn parts like drive cables and connectors are replaced on the manufacturer's schedule, not when they finally fail in the field. The radiographer's job is to find the defect, tag the device, take it out of service, and record it. Daily inspection, maintenance, and use records are retained under the license and inspected by the regulator: a clean record proves the device was verified before use, while a gap in the record is itself a finding. The camera does exactly what it is built to do only as long as every part still works — and the daily check is how you prove that before trusting your life, and your crew's, to a source that never turns off.

figures.title: Gamma-ray exposure device components and inspection chain
Device leakage limits: 200 mR/h surface and 50 mR/h at 1 meter

interactive.title: Pre-Use Inspection: Make the Call
interactive.intro: You are the radiographer opening the truck at 6:30 a.m. to shoot pipe welds. Your iridium-192 camera, drive cable, guide tube, and a calibrated, operating survey meter are in front of you. Work the daily inspection the way a safe radiographer does — and decide when the device stays in service and when it does not.

steps.prompt: Before you handle the camera at all, what is the first thing you do?
options: Connect the drive cable and guide tube so you are ready to shoot
Survey the device with your meter to confirm the source is fully shielded and readings are normal
Open the source lock to check that it moves freely
Check the calibration sticker on the camera and start the log
steps.feedback: You confirm the source is shielded with the meter before you touch or connect anything. A stuck or unshielded source looks identical to a safe one — the instrument is the only honest answer, and it comes first.

steps.prompt: Surveying the locked device, your meter reads 260 mR/h at the surface. The label says the limit is 200 mR/h. What does this tell you?
options: Nothing useful — surface readings always run high and can be ignored
The reading is fine because it is under 500 mR/h
The device is over its legal surface limit; treat it as a shielding or stuck-source problem and take it out of service
Re-zero the meter and average the two readings
steps.feedback: A locked gamma exposure device may read no more than 200 mR/h at the surface. 260 mR/h means the source may not be fully shielded or the shielding is compromised — out of service, do not handle further, and report it.

steps.prompt: A second camera passes the survey, but as you extend the drive cable you feel a gritty catch about halfway and see two broken wire strands. What is the correct action?
options: Lubricate the cable and continue — broken strands are normal wear
Use it for today's short shots only and replace it tonight
Take the device out of service and tag it; a damaged drive cable can disconnect or jam the source
Cut off the frayed section and reattach the connector yourself
steps.feedback: Broken strands, kinks, flat spots, or binding in the drive cable are out-of-service defects. The cable is your only physical control of the source; a failure mid-exposure can leave the source stuck or disconnected. Tag it and route it to qualified maintenance — never field-repair it.

steps.prompt: After your last exposure of the day, you crank the cable back and the crank stops at its normal position. What confirms it is safe to disconnect and pack the camera?
options: The crank reaching its stop is proof the source is locked and shielded
Surveying the device, guide tube, and source-stop with the meter and confirming readings are normal for a shielded device
The status light on the camera shows green
Your partner says the shot looked finished
steps.feedback: The crank stopping is not proof the source returned and locked — pigtails disconnect and cables jam. You survey the device, the full guide tube, and the source-stop and confirm the field is normal before approaching, disconnecting, or packing. The meter is the proof, every time.

checkQuestions.question: What is the maximum radiation level permitted at the exterior surface of a gamma-ray exposure device with the source in the fully shielded (locked) position?
options: 50 millirem per hour
100 millirem per hour
200 millirem per hour
2 rem per hour
checkQuestions.explanation: A locked gamma-ray exposure device may read no more than 200 mR/h at any exterior surface (and no more than 50 mR/h at one meter). A surface reading above 200 mR/h means the device is out of compliance and must be removed from service.

checkQuestions.question: During the daily inspection a radiographer extends the drive cable and finds two broken wire strands and a slight catch at mid-travel. The correct action is to:
options: Apply lubricant and continue, since broken strands are expected wear
Use the device only for the rest of the day, then replace the cable
Remove the device from service, tag it, and route it to qualified maintenance
Trim the damaged section and re-crimp the connector before use
checkQuestions.explanation: A drive cable with broken strands, kinks, flat spots, or binding is an out-of-service defect — the cable is the operator's only physical control of the source, and a failure can leave it stuck or disconnected. Radiographers do not field-repair exposure devices; the camera goes to authorized maintenance.

checkQuestions.question: After cranking the source back at the end of an exposure, what actually confirms the source has returned to the fully shielded position?
options: The crank reaching its normal stop position
A calibrated survey-meter reading of the device, guide tube, and source-stop showing a normal shielded field
The green status indicator on the device
The elapsed exposure timer reaching zero
checkQuestions.explanation: The crank stopping, a status light, or a timer can all be present while a pigtail has disconnected or the source is stuck in the guide tube. Only a survey with a calibrated, operating meter proves the source is shielded — which is why the device, full guide tube, and source-stop are surveyed before anyone approaches.

checkQuestions.question: The radiation leakage limits of 200 mR/h at the surface and 50 mR/h at one meter for a gamma exposure device apply under which condition?
options: While the source is driven out to the exposure position
With the source locked in the fully shielded position
Only during transport on a public highway
Only after the device has been out of service for repair
checkQuestions.explanation: These ceilings describe the maximum acceptable leakage of a properly functioning device with the source fully shielded and locked — not the field during an exposure, which is far higher. Exceeding either value with the source shielded signals a shielding fault or an incompletely retracted source.

checkQuestions.question: Which component physically prevents the source from being driven out the rear of the exposure device during a crank-out?
options: The collimator
The outlet connector
The source-stop assembly
The depleted-uranium shield
checkQuestions.explanation: The source-stop (over-travel stop) assembly limits the source's outward travel so it cannot exit the back of the device. It is checked during the daily inspection; a missing or non-functional source-stop is an out-of-service defect because it allows the source to be driven completely out of control.

references: 10 CFR Part 34 — Licenses for Industrial Radiography and Radiation Safety Requirements (§34.20 performance/leakage limits for exposure devices; daily inspection, maintenance, and operating procedures)
10 CFR Part 20 — Standards for Protection Against Radiation (occupational dose framework and surveys)
ANSI N432 / ANSI N43.9 — Radiological Safety for the Design and Construction of Apparatus for Gamma Radiography
ANSI/ASNT CP-105 (2024) — Radiation Safety topical outline §9 (exposure devices; daily inspection and maintenance; gamma-ray exposure device limits)
